original_abstract:
As global labor markets face increasing volatility due to socio-political instability, rising structural inequality, and precarious employment, international organizations must coordinate policy responses to safeguard worker well-being. This study examines the political dynamics of global agenda setting by analyzing how the International Labour Organization’s (ILO) Decent Work Agenda (DWA) influences the policy landscapes of the World Health Organization (WHO) and the World Bank. 

Through sixteen semi-structured interviews with key informants across these three institutions, the research identifies the drivers and barriers that determine how "decent work" enters and remains on global policy agendas. The findings reveal that the ILO has successfully utilized the DWA to shape a global narrative focused on social justice, which resonates strongly with the WHO’s human rights-based approach to health. Conversely, the World Bank exhibits institutional resistance to the DWA’s specific terminology, preferring market-oriented language such as "job creation" and "access." While the WHO views the agenda as a natural extension of the social determinants of health, World Bank informants highlighted a perceived lack of empirical economic evidence as a barrier to full adoption.

The study also underscores the role of catalytic events—specifically the 2008 global financial crisis—as critical windows of opportunity that allowed the ILO to reassert the importance of social protection in the face of market failure. However, despite successful discursive integration within the UN system via policy coherence initiatives, the practical impact of the DWA at the national level remains constrained by its conceptual nature and the diverse mandates of multi-lateral actors. This research contributes to agenda-setting theory by demonstrating how policy precedents and external shocks interact with institutional ideologies to shape global labor and health governance.
humanized_abstract:
Labor markets are becoming increasingly unstable in the face of growing social and political tensions, income inequality and casualization of labor. It is therefore crucial for international organizations to coordinate their policies in order to protect labor rights. This paper examines the politics of global agenda setting by looking at the influence of the ILO Decent Work Agenda (DWA) on the policy-making of the World Health Organization (WHO) and the World Bank.

This paper discusses the power dynamics at play in the global politics of work and employment, specifically examining the factors that determine the salience of the concept of “decent work” on the global policy agenda. Based on sixteen semi-structured interviews with key stakeholders from the ILO, WHO and World Bank, this research aims to shed light on the factors that affect the inclusion and sustainability of the decent work agenda (DWA) in global policy debates. The findings of this research show that the ILO has managed to promote an agenda on decent work that reflects some of the core principles of social justice, in line with the human rights framework to which the WHO subscribes. In contrast, the language of the DWA does not sit comfortably with the World Bank's market-based discourse, which instead prefers to use terms such as 'job creation' and 'access' to employment. While the DWA is seen as part and parcel of the social determinants of health by stakeholders at the WHO, this agenda is not supported by evidence of its economic impact, at least not to the extent that it has been able to ensure the embedding of this concept in the Bank's policies and practices.

This paper examines how the ILO was able to reassert the centrality of social protection to its mission through a new discourse of decent work. The catalytic events of this process were the agency’s expertise on the consequences of market failure, which surfaced during the financial crisis of 2008. The study also highlights the role of catalytic events; in this case the 2008 global financial crisis. The ILO’s expertise on the social dimensions of market failure was then able to inject a focus on social protection into the UN system through policy coherence initiatives. However, because the DWA is a conceptual instrument, rather than a legally binding agreement with specific national policy implications, its potential to shape national policy was limited by the differing mandates of multilateral organizations. This paper is a major contribution to the field of agenda-setting theory. It shows how a combination of policy precedents and catalytic events interact with the dominant ideology of an institution to determine outcomes in labour and health governance.
